name: Banana Bread
notes: Old family recipe — "Pot Luck" card. Dissolve the soda in boiling water before adding; don't skip this step. Riper the bananas, the better. Walnuts are optional but worth it.
steps: Preheat oven to 350°F. Grease a large loaf pan.
Beat margarine with both sugars until combined. Add beaten eggs and mix well.
Add milk and vanilla. Stir to combine.
Dissolve baking soda in boiling water, then stir into the batter.
Add flour, baking powder, and salt. Mix until just combined — don't overmix.
Fold in mashed bananas and walnuts.
Pour into prepared loaf pan. Bake at 350°F for 40–50 minutes, until a toothpick inserted in the center comes out clean.

name: Steamed BBQ Pork Buns (Char Siu Bao)
notes: Baking powder goes in after the 2-hour rise, not before. Cornstarch is what gives the buns that pillowy dim sum texture. Steam in batches — don't let water touch the buns.

ingredients.name: shallots or red onion, finely chopped

steps: Dissolve yeast in warm water. Mix sifted flour and cornstarch with yeast mixture, sugar, and oil using a dough hook. Knead until smooth. Cover and rest 2 hours.

Make filling: heat oil in wok over medium-high. Stir-fry onion briefly, reduce heat, add sugar, soy sauces, sesame oil. Cook until bubbling. Add stock and flour, stir until thickened. Fold in roast pork. Cool completely.
Add baking powder to risen dough and knead gently until smooth. Add 1–2 tsp water if needed. Rest 15 minutes. Cut ten 4×4-inch parchment squares. Bring steamer to boil.
Roll dough into a log and divide into 10 pieces. Press each into a 4½-inch disc — thicker center, thinner edges. Add filling and pleat until sealed on top.
Place each bun on a parchment square. Steam in two batches, 12 minutes each over high heat.

name: Char Siu (Chinese BBQ Pork)
notes: Needs overnight marinade — plan ahead. Keep fat on the pork for flavor. Use a metal rack so heat circulates. Watch closely after basting; it burns fast. Internal temp 145°F.

ingredients.name: boneless pork shoulder/butt, cut into 2–3 inch strips

steps: Mix sugar, salt, five spice, white pepper, sesame oil, wine, soy sauce, hoisin, molasses, garlic, and food coloring into a marinade. Reserve 2 tbsp separately.
Coat pork strips in remaining marinade. Cover and refrigerate overnight (minimum 8 hours).
Preheat oven to 475°F, rack in upper third. Line a sheet pan with foil, place a metal rack on top. Pour 1½ cups water into the pan below the rack.
Arrange pork on rack with spacing. Roast 25 minutes at 475°F.
Reduce oven to 375°F. Flip pork, add water to pan if dry, rotate pan 180°. Roast another 15 minutes.
Mix reserved marinade with maltose/honey and 1 tbsp hot water for basting sauce.
Baste pork on both sides. Roast final 10 minutes. Optional: broil briefly for extra char — watch carefully.
Baste with remaining sauce. Rest 10 minutes before slicing.

name: Chocolate Cake
notes: Batter will look very runny after adding boiling water - that's ok.
steps: Position rack in center of oven, preheat to 350°F. Spray three 8-inch round cake pans with cooking spray, line bottoms with parchment circles, and spray the parchment.
In a medium bowl, whisk together sugar, flour, cocoa powder, baking powder, baking soda, and salt.
In a large bowl, whisk together the milk, oil, eggs, and vanilla. Gradually whisk in the dry ingredients until just combined.
Slowly pour in the boiling water — batter will be very runny, this is correct. Use a rubber spatula to scrape down the bowl and finish mixing until smooth, 2–3 minutes more.
Divide batter evenly between the three pans (approx. 16 oz / 454g per pan). Bake 30–35 minutes until top springs back when pressed and a skewer comes out with a few crumbs.
Cool completely in pans on a wire rack. Tightly wrap each layer in plastic wrap and freeze overnight (or up to 3 months). End of Day 1.
Sift together confectioners' sugar, cocoa powder, and salt into a medium bowl. In a small cup, whisk together the milk and vanilla.

FROSTING: In a pot on low heat melt butter, vanilla extract, salt, and cocoa. On low, gradually add the dry ingredients. Scrape bowl. Slowly pour in milk and vanilla on low. Scrape bowl again, then beat on medium-high until creamy and smooth, 2–3 minutes. Use immediately.
Cover entire cake with a thin layer of frosting, wet knife to prevent sticking, cool cake in fridge to make firm if struggling.

name: Cong You Bing Tacos
notes: Cant go wrong with the Taiwanese Style Scallion Pancakes at Trader Joes. You want large thin ones - not the complex veggie including ones.
steps: Marinade the asada: In a large bowl. (OOO, salt, pepper, cumin, coriander, cilantro, chili pepper, cayenne, lime juice, orange juice, a little beer if you're drinking one, fresh minced garlic)
Prep the avocado while the asada marinades - slices is best.
Prep the habanero crema - see Habanero Crema recipe or buy from local taqueria.
Pan fry your Cong You Ming on high heat until golden on either side. Slightly under cook them so they're not too crispy for the taco.
*You can do 5 and 6 at the same time* Pan fry asada on medium-high heat, remove and rest for 5-10mins
*You can do 5 and 6 at the same time* Splash a little oil in a pan, add a handful of cheese. Place cong you ming on top of it. Remove when cheese is crispy. Repeat for all cong you ming.
Dress and serve your tacos. YUMMMM

name: Empanada Dough (Fried)
notes:
Recipe from Laylita's Recipes. The dough must be rolled very thin — it cooks fast in the oil and stays fluffy, not bready. Sparkling water gives a lighter texture than still water. Make-ahead tip: assemble the empanadas ahead of time, refrigerate, and fry right before serving.

Savory variant: omit the sugar. Fill with seasoned ground beef, choriqueso, cheese and jalapeño, or mushrooms.

Dessert variant: add 1 tbsp sugar to the dough. Works great with cheese and jam, dulce de leche, or cheese and fruit preserves. Dust with powdered sugar after frying.

ingredients.name: sparkling water (or still water, plus more as needed)

steps: Combine flour, salt, baking powder, and sugar (if using) in a food processor. Pulse briefly to mix.
Add butter pieces and pulse until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs.
Add orange juice and start with 1/2 cup of sparkling water. Pulse and mix, adding more water as needed, until clumps of dough start to form.

Turn dough out onto a surface, form into a ball, and knead lightly until smooth. Place in a bowl, coat the surface with a small amount of oil, cover, and rest at room temperature for 1 hour. (Can also mix and knead entirely by hand — same result.)

To form discs: either roll the dough out into a thin sheet and cut into rounds using a round mold or small plate, or divide dough into 1.5–2 oz balls and press each with a tortilla press or rolling pin. Critical: the discs must be very thin — they cook fast and a thick disc will be doughy inside. Roll each one more if needed.
Fill each disc with about 1 spoonful of filling, placed in the center. Fold over and press edges together with your fingers, then crimp with a fork or twist/fold the edge to seal.
Optional but recommended: chill assembled empanadas for 30–60 minutes before frying. They hold together better and can be made ahead this way.

Heat oil in a deep fryer or pan — enough to cover at least half the empanada. Oil is ready when it bubbles immediately around the empanada. Fry in batches without crowding until golden on each side. Remove any that break or leak immediately.
Drain on paper towels. Serve warm.

name: Roast Beef and Rice Balls
notes: Old family recipe. Start the roast first — it needs a 4-hour head start. The rice balls are essentially oversized arancini and go great served alongside the pulled beef and gravy. For the rice, sushi rice or a half-and-half blend with long grain works best — you need something sticky enough to bind. Fine, powdery breadcrumbs coat much better than coarse ones.

ingredients.name: water, as needed to submerge beef
olive oil or beef tallow (for searing)
sushi rice (or half sushi, half long grain), cooked and cooled

steps: Preheat oven to 350°F. The roast needs to go in first — start here.
Trim excess fat from the chuck roast. If you want, render the trimmed fat down into tallow to use for searing.
Cut the chuck roast into 3–4 large pieces along the fat lines.
Sear all sides of the roast in a hot Dutch oven with oil or tallow, starting with the fatty surfaces. You want a deep brown crust.
Add the cream of mushroom soup and onion soup mix to the Dutch oven. Add just enough water to fully submerge the beef. Stir to combine.
Cover tightly with aluminum foil, then place the lid on top. Bake at 350°F for 3.5 hours, checking occasionally and adding water as needed to keep the beef submerged.
In the last 30 minutes, remove the foil and lid to let the gravy reduce and concentrate. The beef should be fall-apart tender.
While the roast cooks, prepare the rice. Cook, then spread on a sheet pan to cool completely — don't add eggs to warm rice or they'll scramble.
Once cool, mix the cooked rice with the eggs (1 egg per cup of cooked rice) and a good dash of salt and pepper. Toss until evenly combined.

Wet your hands. Form rice into snowball-sized balls — these are big, bigger than a typical arancini. Roll firmly in breadcrumbs, pressing them in to form a solid crust. For extra crunch, double-crumb: dip back in any remaining egg-rice mixture, then roll in crumbs again.
About 45 minutes before the roast comes out, bring the neutral oil to medium frying heat — a stray grain of rice should fizz gently when dropped in. Deep fry rice balls until golden brown all over.
Serve rice balls alongside the pulled roast beef, spooned over with mushroom gravy.

name: Sourdough Starter
notes:
The best way to start is to get a scoop from a friend — an established starter carries years of wild yeast culture and flavor you can't replicate quickly. Starting from scratch: use bread flour at 13%+ protein. Adding 5–10% rye flour to your feed mix is a solid upgrade — rye is rich in wild yeasts and minerals and jumpstarts fermentation.

Flavor builds with age: a fresh starter is mild and yeasty; at 6 months it gets tangy and complex; at a year+ it develops deep sour notes and real character. The older the starter, the more interesting your bread.

Maintenance tools worth having: a proofing/heating mat keeps feeding temps consistent (~75°F is ideal), and a kitchen scale makes hydration ratios precise and repeatable.

Shelf life: at room temp a starter needs daily feeding or it gets over-acidic. In the fridge it can go 1–2 weeks between feedings — pull it out, let it warm up, feed it, put it back. A neglected but not dead starter can usually be revived with a few feedings.

Discard recipes: don't toss the discard — it's great for sourdough pancakes, waffles, crackers, granola, and quick breads.

ingredients.name: bread flour (13%+ protein), or blend with 5–10% rye flour

steps: Day 1: Mix 100g flour and 95g water in a clean jar until no dry flour remains. Cover loosely (not airtight) and leave at room temperature — 70–78°F is ideal. Fresh air exposure helps capture wild yeast.
Days 2–3: You may see bubbles and smell something slightly funky or sour — good sign. Discard all but ~50g, then feed with 100g flour and 95g water. Stir well, cover loosely.

Days 4–7: Feed once or twice daily. The starter should become more active — rising predictably after feedings, full of bubbles, smelling yeasty and pleasantly sour. Ready to bake with when it reliably doubles within 4–8 hours of a feeding and a small spoonful floats in water.
Ongoing: feed on a regular schedule. Room temp requires daily feeding. Fridge storage requires weekly feeding — pull it out the night before baking to wake it up.
To feed: discard all but 50g, add 100g flour and 95g water, stir well, leave until active and bubbly.

name: Ginger Bug
notes:
A ginger bug is a wild-fermented ginger culture — the ginger world's equivalent of a sourdough starter. Use it to naturally carbonate homemade sodas and ginger beer. Leave the skin on the ginger; that's where a lot of the wild yeast lives. Use non-chlorinated water — tap chlorine can stall fermentation.

Shelf life: at room temp, feed daily. In the fridge it can go 1–2 weeks between feedings. A neglected bug gets sour and flat but can usually be revived with a few daily feedings of fresh ginger and sugar — more forgiving than sourdough.

ingredients.name: fresh ginger, grated or finely diced (skin on)

steps: Combine ginger, sugar, and water in a clean jar. Stir well to dissolve the sugar. Cover loosely with a cloth or lid slightly ajar — it needs airflow.
Feed daily: add 1 tbsp fresh ginger (skin on) and 1 tbsp sugar each day. Stir well.
After 5–7 days it should be bubbly and smell fermented and gingery. It's ready when it fizzes actively within a day of feeding.
To use: strain out a portion of the liquid to add to sodas or ginger beer. Replace what you removed with fresh water, ginger, and sugar.
Store at room temp for active use with daily feedings, or refrigerate and feed weekly.

name: Pickled Red Onions
notes: Best at 3–5 days — the onions soften slightly and the brine turns brilliant magenta pink. Good for 2–3 weeks in the fridge. Slice thin; the thinner the cut the faster they pickle and the better the texture. Goes on everything: tacos, burgers, grain bowls, banh mi, eggs.
steps: Slice red onion as thin as possible — a mandoline is ideal.
Pack the sliced onions tightly into a clean jar.
Mix equal parts white vinegar and water with a spoonful of sugar and a dash each of salt and black pepper. Stir to dissolve.
Pour brine over the onions until fully submerged. Press down if needed.
Seal and refrigerate. Ready in a few hours, best after 3–5 days. Keeps 2–3 weeks.

name: Flour Tortillas
notes: Recipe sent to Becca. Roll these thin — almost see-through. That's how you know they're right. They cook very fast; stay at the pan. The 30-minute rest is non-negotiable, it relaxes the gluten so you can actually roll them out.
steps: Combine flour and salt. Cut in butter until mixture resembles coarse crumbs. Add warm water and bring together into a shaggy dough — don't overwork it.
Gently pull the dough together into a ball, then divide into 8 equal pieces. Shape each into a smooth round.
Cover and rest 30 minutes. Don't skip this.
Roll each round out very thin — almost translucent. This is the whole game.
Heat a pan over high heat with a tiny amount of oil. Cook one tortilla at a time, about 20 seconds per side. They go fast.
Stack and cover with a towel to keep warm and pliable.

name: Habanero Mango Salsa
notes: The mango must be very ripe — that sweetness is what balances three habaneros. Charring the peppers is what gives this its smoky backbone. Blend hard until completely smooth. 2 parts vinegar to 1 part water is the ratio — adjust from there for consistency.
steps: Slightly cook the shallot in a dry pan over medium heat to soften it.
In the same pan over high heat, char the habaneros and jalapeños on their sides — you want some real color and smokiness on the skin.
Pull the stems off the charred peppers.
Add everything to a blender: charred peppers, shallot, garlic, mango, olive oil, salt, cumin, vinegar, and water. Dial in the vinegar-to-water ratio to reach your desired consistency.
Blend hard until completely smooth. Taste and adjust salt and cumin.

name: Adobo Crema (Chipotle Crema)
notes: Via Platings and Pairings. Stores well sealed in the fridge for up to 5 days — will thicken as it sits, thin it back out with a splash of lime juice or water. Dial the heat up or down by adjusting the number of chipotle peppers.
steps: Place all ingredients in a blender or food processor.
Blend until completely smooth. Season with salt to taste.
Transfer to a jar or squeeze bottle. Serve immediately or refrigerate until ready.

name: Apple Pie Empanadas (Freedom Pockets 🇺🇸)
notes: Use the Empanada Dough (Fried) recipe with 1 tbsp sugar added. Build the caramelized apple sauce until it's genuinely saucy — that's the hydration source inside the pocket. Brown sugar-cinnamon dust on the outside. Best served with cinnamon ice cream.

ingredients.name: sweet empanada dough (see Empanada Dough recipe — add 1 tbsp sugar)
brown sugar, plus extra for dusting

steps: Make the empanada dough with sugar (see Empanada Dough recipe). Rest 1 hour.
Dice apples small. Cook in a pan with brown sugar, cinnamon, and a pinch of salt over medium heat.

Add splashes of water as needed and keep cooking until apples are very soft and a thick, glossy caramel sauce has built up — you want real sauce, not dry apple bits. This is the hydration inside. Cool completely before filling.
Form and fill empanadas (see dough recipe for technique). One spoonful of apple filling per disc.
Dust the outside with a brown sugar and cinnamon mix before or after frying.
Fry in batches until deep golden. Serve warm alongside cinnamon ice cream.

name: Marinara Sauce
notes: The longer it simmers, the more complex it gets — 2 hours is the target, less is fine but it'll be sweeter. Classic base for pasta, meatballs, pizza, lasagna.

ingredients.name: Italian seasoning (or combo of basil and oregano)

steps: Heat a little olive oil in a stock pot over medium heat. Fry the onions until almost translucent.
Add the garlic and cook another minute until fragrant. Reduce heat.
Add the crushed tomatoes, tomato paste, and all spices. Stir to combine.
Simmer on low for 2 hours, stirring occasionally. Less time is fine but the sauce will be sweeter and less complex.

name: Italian Meatballs
notes: The three-meat combo (beef, veal, pork) is the classic — but beef and turkey works great too. Blending the onion and eggs together in the food processor is the key step; it distributes moisture evenly through the meat. Don't skip it.
steps: Preheat oven to 350°F.
Coarsely chop the onions. Combine with eggs in a food processor and blend about 1 minute until it reaches a slush-like consistency.
In a large bowl, combine all ingredients including the egg-onion mixture. Knead until the consistency is even throughout.
Roll into 1½-inch balls and place on a cookie sheet at least 1 inch apart.
Bake 45 minutes. Makes about 25–30 meatballs.

name: Cinnamon Ice Cream
notes: Based on the Epicurious Vanilla Bean Ice Cream (Gourmet, Sept 1998 — "very good!!", 100% would make again). Adapted to cinnamon. Don't let the custard boil or the eggs scramble. Chilling overnight before churning makes a big difference. The natural pairing for Freedom Pockets.
steps: Combine cream, milk, sugar, cinnamon, and vanilla in a large heavy saucepan. Bring just to a boil, stirring occasionally. Remove from heat.
In a large bowl, lightly beat the eggs. Slowly pour the hot cream mixture into the eggs in a thin stream, whisking constantly — don't rush this or you'll scramble the eggs.
Pour everything back into the saucepan. Cook over moderately low heat, stirring constantly, until a thermometer reads 170°F. Do not let it boil.
Pour through a sieve into a clean bowl. Cool to room temperature, then cover the surface directly with wax paper. Refrigerate at least 3 hours, or overnight.
Churn in an ice cream maker (in 2 batches if needed). Transfer to an airtight container and freeze until firm. Keeps up to 1 week.
